title: Ultra-stable transportable ultraviolet clock laser using cancellation between photo-thermal and photo-birefringence noise
authors: Benjamin Kraus, Sofia Herbers, Constantin Nauk, Uwe Sterr, Christian Lisdat, Piet O. Schmidt
abstract: Optical clocks require an ultra-stable laser to probe and precisely measure the frequency of the narrow-linewidth clock transition. We introduce a portable ultraviolet (UV) laser system for use in an aluminum quantum logic clock, demonstrating a fractional frequency instability of approximately $\mathrm{mod}\,σ_\mathrm{y} = 2 \times 10^{-16}$. The system is based on an ultra-stable cavity with crystalline AlGaAs/GaAs mirror coatings, alongside with a frequency quadrupling system employing two single-pass second harmonic generation (SHG) stages. Its acceleration sensitivity, measured in all three axes, does not exceed $4(2) \times 10^{-12}$/(ms$^{-2}$) and is among the lowest recorded for transportable systems to date. Additionally, partial cancellation between photo-thermal noise and photo-birefringence noise is used to effectively mitigate noise induced by intra-cavity optical power fluctuation at lower Fourier frequencies.
summary: The paper presents a highly stable, transportable ultraviolet laser system designed for aluminum quantum logic clocks. By leveraging the opposite signs of photo-thermal and photo-birefringence noise, the researchers achieved significant noise cancellation. This advancement enables more robust and portable optical frequency standards for applications like geodesy and searches for new physics.
main_problem: The need for ultra-stable, transportable ultraviolet (UV) laser systems to probe narrow-linewidth clock transitions in aluminum quantum logic clocks, specifically addressing frequency instability caused by the interplay between photo-thermal and photo-birefringence noise.
main_result: Demonstrated a portable UV laser system with a fractional frequency instability of approximately 2 x 10^-16 at 1 s, achieving noise mitigation through the partial cancellation of photo-thermal and photo-birefringence effects.
method: The researchers used a frequency quadrupling system (two SHG stages) on a 1070 nm seed laser and analyzed the frequency response to power fluctuations using step changes and sinusoidal modulation of intra-cavity optical power.
model_or_system: A portable UV laser system operating at 267.4 nm, utilizing an ultra-stable 20 cm cavity with crystalline AlGaAs/GaAs mirror coatings on fused silica substrates and ULE glass components.
key_observables: Fractional frequency instability (sigma_y), Power Spectral Density (PSD) of fractional frequency noise, acceleration sensitivity, and optical path length response.
important_parameters: Fractional frequency instability of 2 x 10^-16 at 1 s; cavity finesse of 2.02 x 10^5; UV output power up to 60 microWatts; acceleration sensitivity as low as 1 x 10^-12/(ms^-2).
figures_summary: Figure 4 compares the optical length response for fast and slow polarization axes and breaks down photo-thermal noise components; Figure 5 shows the response to sinusoidal modulation at different frequencies; Figure 6 compares the PSD of frequency noise against various noise sources and shows Allan deviation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the requirement for UV clock lasers, describes the experimental setup (cavity, frequency quadrupling, and isolation), details the measurement of power-induced noise and its cancellation mechanism, and concludes with a performance comparison against existing stability limits.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and AMO physics as it demonstrates advanced techniques for controlling laser noise and stability, which are critical for the precision required in quantum logic clocks and fundamental physics tests.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: 3D-model of the cavity, including the mirrors (blue), the ULE spacer (yellow), and the suspension attached to the mounting frame (green).

Figure 2: Calculated power spectral density (PSD) Sy of fractional frequency instability induced by mechanical vibration, while the cavity is placed on a passive vibration isolation platform (left), on an active platform (middle), and on the active platform placed inside the rack (right). Legend: Vibration-induced frequency noise along the optical axis (red), horizontally and perpendicular to the optical axis (green) and vertical (black). For comparison the thermal noise limit is shown (brow).

Figure 3: Response of the resonance frequency of the cavity to a step function of the optical intra-cavity power for the fast polarization axis (brown) and for the slow polarization axis (orange). Summing the frequency response of both polarization modes eliminates the birefringence effect and represents the photo-thermal effects (gray). Subtracting the frequency response for the slow axis from the frequency response for the fast axis shows the birefringence effect on the polarization line splitting (black). The birefringence effect shows a faster response to power changes than the photo-thermal effects. The optical power modulation is shown for the fast polarization axis (dark blue) and...

Figure 4: Modulus of the response of the optical length of the cavity to a sinusoidal modulation of the optical intra-cavity power for the fast polarization axis with mean optical intra-cavity power of 0.4 W (green), 0.7 W (blue) and 1.8 W (orange) and for slow polarization axis with mean optical intra-cavity power of 0.4 W (red). The magnitude of the transfer function is shown for the total photo-thermal noise (brown), which is the sum of the photo-thermoelastic contribution of the substrate (green) and the photo-thermal contribution of the coating (solid blue) Farsi et al. [2012], assuming an absorption coefficient of 2 ppm for each mirror. The photo-thermal contribution of the coating...

Figure 5: Response of the optical length of the cavity to a sinusoidal modulation of the optical intra-cavity power for the fast polarization axis with a modulation frequency of 0.5 Hz (blue), for the slow polarization axis with a modulation frequency of 0.5 Hz (green) and with a modulation frequency of 0.05 Hz (orange).
